meta.title: F10 M5 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every BMW M5 F10 engine – S63B44, S63B44TU, S63B44TU (30 Jahre) and S63B44B (Competition Package). Real UK costs and honest reliability data. Get free quotes from 20+ vetted BMW specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW M5 F10 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned F10 M5 engine typically costs £6,000–£12,000 supply only [BMW-QUOTE], depending on the engine – the standard S63B44 sits at £6,000–£10,000, while the Competition Package's S63B44B can exceed £12,000. Fitting adds £1,040–£1,430 at independent specialists. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

mainEntity.name: Which F10 M5 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The S63B44TU (LCI, 2013–2016) is the most reliable F10 M5 engine – the Technical Update addressed many of the early engine's weaknesses including revised rod bearing specifications, improved cooling system components, and updated engine management. The Competition Package engine is also reliable but carries additional stress from the higher power output.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW M5 F10?

acceptedAnswer.text: Rod bearing wear on all S63 engines (the most critical issue), high-pressure fuel pump and injector failure (leading to oil dilution), cooling system weaknesses (water pump, radiators), ignition coil failures, and valve stem seal wear on higher-mileage examples.

mainEntity.name: Is the BMW M5 F10 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The F10 M5 is a reliable car for what it is – a 560 hp twin-turbo V8 – but it has well-documented weak points that must be addressed proactively. The S63 is fundamentally robust, but rod bearings are a known weakness that requires preventative replacement. The fuel system and cooling system also demand attention. With diligent maintenance, the F10 M5 is a durable and rewarding car.

mainEntity.name: Which F10 M5 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: Early S63B44 engines (2011–2013, pre-LCI) without documented rod bearing and fuel system work – failure can be catastrophic. If you're not prepared for the maintenance bills, stick with a post-LCI car (2013–2016) with documented service history. Competition Package cars are desirable but command a premium.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my F10 M5 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Rod bearing wear may not be immediately obvious until failure occurs – regular oil analysis is the best preventative measure. Fuel system issues (hard starting, rough idle), misfires (ignition coils), overheating (cooling system), or blue smoke on startup (valve stem seals) are the key warning signs. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a BMW M5 F10 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Apply the 70% BMW scrap threshold. Against a well-kept £25,000 F10 M5, a £6,000–£10,000 S63 recon is 24–40% – safe. Against a tired £15,000 example, the same recon is 40–67% – borderline. For the Competition Package, values are high enough that even a £12,000 S63B44B rebuild is often a viable investment.

mainEntity.name: How long do F10 M5 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: The S63 can achieve 150,000+ miles with proper maintenance – but rod bearing replacement is essential at 50,000 miles. The fuel system (HPFP, injectors) should be inspected regularly, and oil changes should be performed every 6,000 miles (not BMW's recommended interval). With the bearings and fuel system addressed, the S63 is a durable, high-mileage engine.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the F10 M5 and the standard F10 5 Series?

acceptedAnswer.text: The F10 M5 is the high-performance variant of the F10 5 Series, powered by the 4.4-litre S63 twin-turbo V8 producing 560–600 hp. It features unique body panels (wider front wings, different bumpers, bonnet with power bulge), a strengthened chassis, M-specific suspension, brakes, and a 7-speed dual-clutch transmission. The M5 was the first turbocharged M5, replacing the E60's V10.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my F10 M5 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate (visible through the windscreen) and vehicle documentation. The engine code is stamped on the cylinder block – on the S63, it's located on the left-hand side of the block. Tell us your variant (standard M5, Competition Package, or 30 Jahre) and year (pre-/post-2013 LCI) and we'll identify it – 20+ vetted BMW specialists will confirm it for free.

hero.h1: BMW M5 F10 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the F10 M5 — from the 560 hp S63B44 twin-turbo V8 to the 600 hp Competition Package and the 575 hp 30 Jahre special. Real UK replacement costs, honest reliability data, and the rod bearing and cooling system weaknesses that define this generation's ownership story. Backed by real UK F10 M5 enquiries in 2025 [BMW-VERIFIED].

engineDatabase.h2: BMW M5 F10 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the F10 M5 — from the standard S63B44 to the Competition Package and 30 Jahre variants.

overview.intro: The BMW F10 M5, produced from 2011 to 2016, is the fifth generation of BMW's legendary M5 and the first to feature a turbocharged engine. Powered by the 4.4-litre S63 twin-turbo V8 producing 560 hp, it replaced the high-revving V10 of the E60 with a more muscular, torque-rich powerplant that delivered 680 Nm from just 1,500 rpm. The F10 M5 set new benchmarks for luxury performance saloons, combining supercar-rivalling pace with the comfort and practicality expected of a 5 Series.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: BMW L6 (F10/F11) • Predecessor: BMW M5 E60 • Successor: BMW M5 F90 • Sibling body styles in this generation: None (F10 M5 was saloon only; M5 Touring not offered in this generation)
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The F10 M5 generated real UK engine enquiries in 2025 [BMW-VERIFIED], with the S63B44TU and Competition Package accounting for the majority of demand as these cars enter second and third ownership cycles.

items.engine: S63B44TU (LCI, 2013–2016) – "The Technical Update version of the S63 addressed many of the early engine's weaknesses — revised rod bearing specifications, improved cooling system components, and updated engine management. It's the sweet spot of the F10 M5 range — 560 hp with better reliability than the early cars."
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want F10 M5 performance with the benefit of BMW's running improvements.

items.engine: S63B44 (pre-LCI, 2011–2013) – "Early S63 engines carry a documented rod bearing wear risk — failure can occur as early as 60,000 miles, leading to a £10,000+ engine replacement. The S63's rod bearings are not as notorious as the S85 V10, but they are a genuine concern. Oil dilution from injector or HPFP failure is another risk."
items.whoItsFor: only buyers who have confirmed rod bearing replacement or are budgeting for it.

items.engine: S63B44B (Competition Package) – "The Competition Package's 600 hp engine is the most powerful F10 M5 variant — but that extra power comes with extra stress on the rod bearings, turbochargers and cooling system. A full replacement is the most expensive bill in the F10 range."
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want the ultimate F10 M5 and accept the associated running costs.

items.engine: S63B44TU (LCI, non-Competition) – "The LCI engine delivers 560 hp with improved reliability over early cars, without the Competition Package's additional complexity and running costs. It's the smart choice for most F10 M5 buyers."
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want proven F10 M5 performance without the Comp Pack premium.

items.engine: S63B44TU (30 Jahre M5) – "The 575 hp 30 Jahre special is often overlooked in favour of the Competition Package, but it delivers genuine collector appeal with only 300 units built worldwide. The engine is effectively a detuned Comp Pack unit with unique badging."
items.whoItsFor: collectors and enthusiasts who want a rare F10 M5 variant.

rows.ownershipVerdict: High Risk — bearings and fuel system are the big ones; budget accordingly.
Solid — improved bearings; still need preventative care.
Collectable — rare and desirable; specialist maintenance required.
Expensive — 600 hp comes with 600 hp bills.

economicsRule.title: The F10 M5 rule
economicsRule.text: A £6,000–£10,000 S63 recon against a £15,000–£30,000 F10 M5 sits at 20–67% of the car's value. On a sub-£18,000 example, that brushes our 70% BMW threshold — we'll honestly tell you to walk away unless the car has documented rod bearing work. Against a £25,000+ low-mileage example, the same recon drops to a routine 24–40% — a repair decision, not a financial one. For the Competition Package, values are high enough that even a £12,000 S63B44B rebuild is often a viable investment.

keyTakeaways.question: Which F10 M5 Costs Least To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: S63B44TU (LCI) — improved bearings, no Comp Pack complexity.

keyTakeaways.question: Which F10 M5 Costs Most To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: S63B44B (Competition Package) — 600 hp with premium running costs.

keyTakeaways.question: Which F10 M5 Is Best Value?
keyTakeaways.answer: S63B44TU (LCI) — the sweet spot between performance, reliability and value.

problems.title: S63 Rod Bearing Wear (All S63 Engines)
problems.rootCause: The S63's rod bearings can wear prematurely, particularly in early engines and those subjected to repeated high-RPM use. A teardown of a 103,000-mile S63 revealed heavily worn rod bearings alongside a spun crank bearing. Oil dilution from injector or HPFP failure can accelerate bearing wear. Symptoms include a knocking sound at specific engine speeds and metallic debris in the oil.
problems.recommendation: If you own an S63-powered F10 M5, budget for rod bearing replacement at 50,000 miles — £1,500–£3,000 now is dramatically cheaper than the £6,000+ replacement that follows a failure. Use ARP bolts and high-quality bearings. Pre-LCI cars are the highest risk; LCI cars have improved specs but are not immune. Do not follow BMW's recommended oil change intervals — change oil every 6,000 miles.

cta.label: Compare S63 rod bearing replacement quotes for your BMW M5 F10 →

problems.title: High-Pressure Fuel Pump & Injector Failure
problems.rootCause: The S63's high-pressure fuel pumps and injectors can fail, leading to fuel dilution of the engine oil — which accelerates bearing wear and can grenade the engine. Symptoms include hard starting, rough idle, loss of power and misfires.
problems.recommendation: If your F10 M5 shows any hard starting, rough idle or misfires, have the fuel system inspected immediately — £800–£1,500 now is far cheaper than the £6,000+ replacement that follows a catastrophic failure. Change the oil after any fuel system work to remove contaminated oil.

cta.label: Compare S63 fuel system repair quotes for your BMW M5 F10 →

problems.title: Cooling System Weaknesses (All S63 Engines)
problems.rootCause: The S63's high thermal load necessitates an efficient cooling system — weak points like water pumps and radiators can cause overheating if not addressed. The hot-vee design places turbos in the valley of the engine, generating significant heat.
problems.recommendation: On any F10 M5, inspect the cooling system annually and replace the water pump proactively at 50,000–60,000 miles — £600–£1,200 now is cheap insurance against a £6,000+ replacement. Use the correct BMW coolant and change it every two years.

cta.label: Compare S63 cooling system repair quotes for your BMW M5 F10 →

problems.title: Ignition Coil Failures
problems.rootCause: High-performance turbocharged engines like the S63 place significant stress on the ignition system — coils can fail over time. A single failed coil often signals that others will follow. Symptoms include misfires, rough idling and reduced power.
problems.recommendation: If your F10 M5 has a misfire, replace all ignition coils and spark plugs at the same time — £300–£600 now is far cheaper than the engine damage that can follow prolonged misfires. Use genuine BMW or high-quality OEM coils.

cta.label: Compare S63 ignition coil replacement quotes for your BMW M5 F10 →

problems.title: Valve Stem Seal Wear
problems.rootCause: Over time, valve stem seals can wear and cause oil consumption, particularly in engines with higher mileage. Symptoms include blue smoke on startup and increased oil consumption between services.
problems.recommendation: If your F10 M5 is consuming oil between services, have the valve stem seals inspected — £1,000–£2,000 now is far cheaper than the £6,000+ replacement that follows oil starvation. Use the correct oil spec and change oil regularly.

cta.label: Compare S63 valve stem seal repair quotes for your BMW M5 F10 →
